═══ [1] ═══════════════════════════════════════
HEADLINE: Saudi Aramco Shuts Ras Tanura Refinery After Iran's Drone Attacks
SOURCE: www.ndtv.com
PUBLISHED: 2026-03-11 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.ndtv.com/india-news/iran-israel-war-iran-conflict-iran-news-saudi-aramco-shuts-ras-tanura-refinery-after-irans-drone-attacks-11158043

Saudi Aramco Shuts Ras Tanura Refinery After Iran's Drone Attacks

New Delhi:
Saudi Aramco shut its Ras Tanura refinery after Iran's drone attacks, marking a sharp escalation on the third day of strikes launched by Tehran in response to the US-Israeli attack on Iran. The state oil giant halted operations at the Saudi Aramco-owned Ras Tanura refinery after the facility was hit by a drone on Monday. The complex, located on Saudi Arabia's Gulf coast, is one of the Middle East's largest refineries with a capacity of 550,000 barrels per day (bpd) and serves as a critical export terminal for Saudi crude. Ras Tanura handles a significant share of the kingdom's oil exports, with shipments typically heading to major markets in Europe and Asia, including China, Japan and South Korea. Follow LIVE Upates Here
The refinery was shut as a precautionary measure and the situation is under control, a source told Reuters. Two drones were intercepted at the facility, with debris causing a limited fire, according to the Saudi defence ministry's spokesperson speaking on Al Arabiya TV. There were no injuries. Aramco, on the other hand, is yet to issue a statement over the attack. The shutdown is likely to heighten supply concerns as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz - through which roughly a fifth of global oil consumption flows - slowed to a near-halt after vessels were attacked in the area on Sunday. Brent crude futures surged about 10% on Monday. "The attack on Saudi Arabia's Ras Tanura refinery marks a significant escalation, with Gulf energy infrastructure now squarely in Iran's sights," said Torbjorn Soltvedt, Principal Middle East Analyst at risk intelligence firm Verisk Maplecroft. The drone strike forms part of a broader wave of attacks across the region, including reported strikes on Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Doha, Manama and Oman's commercial port of Duqm. In Iraq, most oil production in the Kurdistan region - which exported around 200,000 bpd to Turkey in February - was shut over the weekend as a precaution, according to field operators.

HEADLINE: Saudi Aramco shuts Ras Tanura refinery after drone strike
SOURCE: www.bairdmaritime.com
PUBLISHED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.bairdmaritime.com/shipping/ports/saudi-aramco-shuts-ras-tanura-refinery-after-drone-strike

Saudi Aramco shuts Ras Tanura refinery after drone strike

Saudi Aramco shut its Ras Tanura refinery after it was hit by a drone, an industry source said on Monday, an apparent escalation on the third day of strikes across the region launched by Tehran in response to the US-Israeli attack on Iran. The Ras Tanura complex, on the kingdom's Persian Gulf coast, houses one of the Middle East's largest refineries with a capacity of 550,000 barrels per day (bpd) and serves as a critical export terminal for Saudi crude. Ras Tanura was shut as a precautionary measure and the situation is under control, the source said. Two drones were intercepted at the facility, with debris causing a limited fire, the Saudi defence ministry's spokesperson said on Al Arabiya TV, adding there were no injuries. Its shuttering will likely add to supply anxieties as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, through which around a fifth of global oil consumption flows, grinds to a near-halt after vessels were attacked around it on Sunday. Brent crude futures surged roughly 10 per cent on Monday. "The attack on Saudi Arabia’s Ras Tanura refinery marks a significant escalation, with gulf energy infrastructure now squarely in Iran’s sights," said Torbjorn Soltvedt, Principal Middle East Analyst at risk intelligence firm Verisk Maplecroft. "The attack is also likely to move Saudi Arabia and neighbouring gulf states closer to joining US and Israeli military operations against Iran."
Aramco did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment. The drone strike added to a wave of attacks on the region, including on Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Doha, Manama and Oman's commercial port of Duqm. Most of oil production in the Kurdistan region of Iraq, which exported around 200,000 bpd in February to Turkey, was shut down over the weekend as a precaution, field operators said.

HEADLINE: Iran hits Saudi Aramco’s Ras Tanura oil refinery; IRGC claims it 'opened massive gates of fire'
SOURCE: www.hindustantimes.com
PUBLISHED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.hindustantimes.com/world-news/iran-hits-saudi-aramco-s-ras-tanura-oil-refinery-irgc-claims-it-opened-massive-gates-of-fire-101772442112999.html

Iran hits Saudi Aramco’s Ras Tanura oil refinery; IRGC claims it 'opened massive gates of fire'

The conflict between the United States-Israel alliance and the Iranian regime intensified on Monday, with Tehran launching a new round of missile and drone strikes on Israel and other Gulf states. Multiple media outlets, including RT, Press TV and i24NEWS, reported a drone strike on Saudi Aramco’s Ras Tanura refining facility. The site is one of the world’s largest oil export hubs and plays a crucial role in global crude supply. The Jerusalem Post, quoting a reporter, said a small and isolated fire had broken out at the Ras Tanura facility but was later brought under control. Iran's Press TV also reported an Iranian missile strike on Salman Port in Bahrain. Iranian media claimed the port had been used to transport American logistical equipment intended for operations against Iran. IRGC statement and media claims
In a statement, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) provided an update on what it has called Operation True Promise. According to the statement, “The tenth wave opened massive gates of fire upon the occupied territories with a maneuver of Khaibar missiles.”
Iranian state media also shared an image on Telegram that it alleged showed an American F-15 pilot whose aircraft was downed in Kuwait earlier on Monday. Press TV posted on Telegram: “American pilot after his fighter jet crashed in Kuwait”. Visuals were also circulated showing what Iranian outlets described as a large drone fleet and strikes on US-Israeli targets across the region. Amichai Stein, diplomatic correspondent for i24NEWS, said Gulf officials were taken aback by the scale of Iran’s retaliation. In a post on X, he wrote, “Out of all my conversations with senior officials in the Gulf states, they were completely shocked by the intensity of the Iranian fire. "We were certain that after the elimination of Khamenei they would respond, but right now they're aiming at population centers, not just military facilities; this is completely insane and this is conduct that makes no sense on their part," one of them told me.

Major Saudi refinery, Kurdish and Israeli oil, gas fields shut amid Mid-East strikes

Attack seen as significant escalation
“The attack on Saudi Arabia’s Ras Tanura refinery marks a significant escalation, with Gulf energy infrastructure now squarely in Iran’s sights,” said Mr Torbjorn Soltvedt, principal Middle East analyst at risk intelligence firm Verisk Maplecroft. “The attack is also likely to move Saudi Arabia and neighbouring Gulf states closer to joining US and Israeli military operations against Iran.”
Saudi Arabia’s heavily fortified energy facilities have been targeted previously, most notably in September 2019 when drone and missile attacks on the Abqaiq and Khurais plants temporarily knocked out more than half of the kingdom’s crude production. Saudi Aramco Shuts Ras Tanura Refinery After Iran's Drone Attacks

New Delhi:
Saudi Aramco shut its Ras Tanura refinery after Iran's drone attacks, marking a sharp escalation on the third day of strikes launched by Tehran in response to the US-Israeli attack on Iran. The state oil giant halted operations at the Saudi Aramco-owned Ras Tanura refinery after the facility was hit by a drone on Monday. The complex, located on Saudi Arabia's Gulf coast, is one of the Middle East's largest refineries with a capacity of 550,000 barrels per day (bpd) and serves as a critical export terminal for Saudi crude. Ras Tanura handles a significant share of the kingdom's oil exports, with shipments typically heading to major markets in Europe and Asia, including China, Japan and South Korea. Follow LIVE Upates Here
The refinery was shut as a precautionary measure and the situation is under control, a source told Reuters. Two drones were intercepted at the facility, with debris causing a limited fire, according to the Saudi defence ministry's spokesperson speaking on Al Arabiya TV. There were no injuries. Aramco, on the other hand, is yet to issue a statement over the attack. The shutdown is likely to heighten supply concerns as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz - through which roughly a fifth of global oil consumption flows - slowed to a near-halt after vessels were attacked in the area on Sunday. Brent crude futures surged about 10% on Monday. "The attack on Saudi Arabia's Ras Tanura refinery marks a significant escalation, with Gulf energy infrastructure now squarely in Iran's sights," said Torbjorn Soltvedt, Principal Middle East Analyst at risk intelligence firm Verisk Maplecroft. The drone strike forms part of a broader wave of attacks across the region, including reported strikes on Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Doha, Manama and Oman's commercial port of Duqm. In Iraq, most oil production in the Kurdistan region - which exported around 200,000 bpd to Turkey in February - was shut over the weekend as a precaution, according to field operators.

Saudi Aramco shuts Ras Tanura refinery after drone strike

Saudi Aramco shut its Ras Tanura refinery after it was hit by a drone, an industry source said on Monday, an apparent escalation on the third day of strikes across the region launched by Tehran in response to the US-Israeli attack on Iran. The Ras Tanura complex, on the kingdom's Persian Gulf coast, houses one of the Middle East's largest refineries with a capacity of 550,000 barrels per day (bpd) and serves as a critical export terminal for Saudi crude. Ras Tanura was shut as a precautionary measure and the situation is under control, the source said. Two drones were intercepted at the facility, with debris causing a limited fire, the Saudi defence ministry's spokesperson said on Al Arabiya TV, adding there were no injuries. Its shuttering will likely add to supply anxieties as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, through which around a fifth of global oil consumption flows, grinds to a near-halt after vessels were attacked around it on Sunday. Brent crude futures surged roughly 10 per cent on Monday. "The attack on Saudi Arabia’s Ras Tanura refinery marks a significant escalation, with gulf energy infrastructure now squarely in Iran’s sights," said Torbjorn Soltvedt, Principal Middle East Analyst at risk intelligence firm Verisk Maplecroft. "The attack is also likely to move Saudi Arabia and neighbouring gulf states closer to joining US and Israeli military operations against Iran."
Aramco did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment. The drone strike added to a wave of attacks on the region, including on Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Doha, Manama and Oman's commercial port of Duqm. Most of oil production in the Kurdistan region of Iraq, which exported around 200,000 bpd in February to Turkey, was shut down over the weekend as a precaution, field operators said.

Iran hits Saudi Aramco’s Ras Tanura oil refinery; IRGC claims it 'opened massive gates of fire'

The conflict between the United States-Israel alliance and the Iranian regime intensified on Monday, with Tehran launching a new round of missile and drone strikes on Israel and other Gulf states. Multiple media outlets, including RT, Press TV and i24NEWS, reported a drone strike on Saudi Aramco’s Ras Tanura refining facility. The site is one of the world’s largest oil export hubs and plays a crucial role in global crude supply. The Jerusalem Post, quoting a reporter, said a small and isolated fire had broken out at the Ras Tanura facility but was later brought under control. Iran's Press TV also reported an Iranian missile strike on Salman Port in Bahrain. Iranian media claimed the port had been used to transport American logistical equipment intended for operations against Iran. IRGC statement and media claims
In a statement, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) provided an update on what it has called Operation True Promise. According to the statement, “The tenth wave opened massive gates of fire upon the occupied territories with a maneuver of Khaibar missiles.”
Iranian state media also shared an image on Telegram that it alleged showed an American F-15 pilot whose aircraft was downed in Kuwait earlier on Monday. Press TV posted on Telegram: “American pilot after his fighter jet crashed in Kuwait”. Visuals were also circulated showing what Iranian outlets described as a large drone fleet and strikes on US-Israeli targets across the region. Amichai Stein, diplomatic correspondent for i24NEWS, said Gulf officials were taken aback by the scale of Iran’s retaliation. In a post on X, he wrote, “Out of all my conversations with senior officials in the Gulf states, they were completely shocked by the intensity of the Iranian fire. "We were certain that after the elimination of Khamenei they would respond, but right now they're aiming at population centers, not just military facilities; this is completely insane and this is conduct that makes no sense on their part," one of them told me.

HEADLINE: Balen Shah: Rapper, mayor, Nepal’s next prime minister?
SOURCE: www.aljazeera.com
PUBLISHED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.aljazeera.com/features/2026/3/2/balen-shah-rapper-mayor-nepals-next-prime-minister

Balen Shah: Rapper, mayor, Nepal’s next prime minister?

Kathmandu, Nepal – Facing thousands of raucous supporters, 35-year-old Balendra Shah lifted his signature black rectangular sunglasses, asked his audience to look him in the eye, and said: “I love you.”
It is a sentiment that millions of young Nepalis appear to reciprocate. Balen – as he is popularly known – was a nobody until 2013, when he almost overnight became a rap sensation. Nearly a decade later, in May 2022, he stunned Nepal’s deeply entrenched mainstream political parties by winning the post of mayor of Kathmandu, the country’s capital, while contesting as an independent. When the Himalayan nation of 30 million people erupted in popular protests against the government of then-Prime Minister KP Sharma Oli in September 2025, Balen emerged as a high-profile backer of the protesters. He was the first choice of many Gen Z activists to take over as interim leader after Oli was forced to resign. But he instead supported former Supreme Court Chief Justice Sushila Karki for the post. It is now that this was a tactical move. As Nepal heads to its first election since the protests last year, and Karki’s brief term ends, Balen is positioning himself as the future prime minister the country needs. And true to style, he is doing it with a bang: He is contesting the parliamentary elections from Jhapa-5, a seat about 300km (186 miles) southeast of Kathmandu, against Oli, the man protesters deposed just five months ago. On the surface, the odds appear stacked against him. The region is a stronghold of Oli and the Communist Party of Nepal – Unified Marxist Leninist (CPN-UML), which the former prime minister heads. Balen is contesting as a candidate of the Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP), a centrist party formed less than four years ago, which won 10 percent of the national vote in the last elections in 2022.

HEADLINE: Indian killed as projectile hits MKD VYOM; Embassy in Oman offers condolences
SOURCE: www.khaleejtimes.com
PUBLISHED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.khaleejtimes.com/world/gulf/indian-killed-projectile-hits-mkd-vyom-oman

Indian killed as projectile hits MKD VYOM; Embassy in Oman offers condolences

[Editor's Note: Follow Khaleej Times live blog amid Israeli, US strikes on Iran for the latest regional developments.]
The Embassy of India on Monday expressed its deepest condolences after the death of an Indian national on board the vessel MKD VYOM. The fire resulted from a projectile hitting the tanker amid rising regional conflict following US and Israeli strikes on Iran and Tehran’s retaliation across the Middle East. The Embassy confirmed that it is coordinating closely with local authorities in Oman to facilitate the safe and early repatriation of Indian nationals on board the ship. Officials emphasised that the Embassy remains committed to providing all possible assistance in this matter. Stay up to date with the latest news. Follow KT on WhatsApp Channels. On Sunday, the manager of the ship said that a projectile hit the Marshall Islands–flagged product tanker MKD VYOM killing a crew member on board as the vessel sailed off the coast of Oman. "The vessel suffered an explosion and subsequent fire after being struck by a suspected projectile while off the coast of Muscat, Oman on 1 March," V.Ships Asia said in a statement. "It is with great sadness that we confirm one crew member, who was in the engine room at the time of the incident, has died."
The Duqm commercial port was targeted by two drones on Sunday, one of which struck a mobile workers' accommodation, the Oman News Agency reported. A security source reported that, due to the strike on the accommodation, an expatriate worker was injured. According to the statement, debris from the other fell into an area near the fuel tanks; however, there were no human or material losses.

HEADLINE: First Indian casualty in US-Iran conflict, Oman says mariner killed as oil tanker hit by bomb via drone boat
SOURCE: www.hindustantimes.com
PUBLISHED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00 | DOWNLOADED: 2026-03-02 00:00:00
URL: https://www.hindustantimes.com/india-news/first-indian-casualty-in-us-iran-conflict-oman-says-mariner-killed-as-tanker-hit-101772448707419.html

First Indian casualty in US-Iran conflict, Oman says mariner killed as oil tanker hit by bomb via drone boat

It was not immediately reported who launched this attack amid the US-Israel strikes on Iran and the latter's retaliation on US bases across the Middle East/West Asia region. The state-run Oman News Agency reported the attack in the Gulf of Oman off the coast of Muscat, the sultanate's capital. It identified the vessel as the MKD VYOM. It said the dead crew member was from India, news agency AP reported. Here: Track live updates on the conflict
Reports were in on Sunday already from news agency Reuters about a projectile hitting tanker MKD VYOM. That killed a crew member on board as the vessel sailed off the coast of Oman, its manager V.Ships said on Sunday. The victim was not identified, and now their nationality has been reported. "The vessel suffered an explosion and subsequent fire after being struck by a suspected projectile while off the coast of Muscat, Oman on 1 March," V.Ships Asia said in a statement on Sunday. "It is with great sadness that we confirm one crew member, who was in the engine room at the time of the incident, has died," it added. Iranian foreign minister Abbas Araghchi on Monday responded to questions on Iranian strikes across Gulf countries, even as explosions were reported in Dubai, Doha, Manama, and the Omani port of Duqm for a second consecutive day. Speaking to Al Jazeera, Aragchi specifically elaborated on the strike in the Duqm port in Oman, which is playing mediator between Iran and US-Israel. He said that the attack was not the regime's choice, adding that the armed forces were now “acting independently” based on general instructions. Oman’s maritime security centre did not specify what hit the vessel. The attack followed earlier drone strikes elsewhere in the Gulf country, at the commercial port of Duqm on the Arabian Sea. These incidents marked the first time targets in or near Oman have been hit following a wave of retaliatory strikes by Tehran on Gulf states after joint US-Israeli attacks on Iran that have plunged the region into a new war.

-rw-rw-r--+ 1 3646 1040 190516 Apr 30 05:39 2025-12-24_notes.md
- Newcastle UCL opponent resolved to Barcelona, matching the two-outcome Barcelona/Chelsea forecast.

Updates made:
- South Africa T20 semifinal opponent: South Africa beat West Indies and India beat Zimbabwe, making South Africa effectively secure from Group 1 and leaving Group 2 as mostly the England-New Zealand loser, with Pakistan only a large-NRR miracle path. Submitted New Zealand 0.53, England 0.44, Pakistan 0.025.
- US-Iran final-collapse venue: Al Jazeera and Reuters-derived reporting said Geneva talks ended with claimed progress, no clear collapse, and Vienna technical talks scheduled next week. Cut Geneva and added substantial Vienna mass.
- US base-access/launch-area questions: Hindustan Times/WSJ-derived reporting said F-22s deployed to Israeli bases for a potential Iran mission, while Saudi Arabia and the UAE refused US use of airspace/territory. Raised Saudi Arabia and UAE for the trade-cutoff/base-access threat, and added Israel/Jordan for the launch-area question.
- Prediction-market bill co-sponsor: the Chris Murphy resolution on the related "ASAP" market makes him more likely than Elizabeth Warren to be the senator paired with Jeff Merkley. Raised Murphy.
- Nepal prime minister: Reuters/Straits Times described Balendra Shah and the Rastriya Swatantra Party as frontrunners before the March 5 election, with Gagan Thapa, K.P. Sharma Oli and Pushpa Kamal Dahal as other contenders. Replaced the old Deuba/Dahal-heavy forecast.
- DHS replacement: Daily Beast/Semafor reporting said White House insiders want Kristi Noem out and that Tom Homan has already taken operational control in a high-profile immigration matter. Raised Homan and added Troy Edgar as a plausible institutional replacement.
- Pakistan-Afghanistan drone-target city: Feb. 26 border-clash reporting named multiple Khyber Pakhtunkhwa sectors after Pakistani strikes on Nangarhar and Paktia. Added Peshawar while keeping Lahore/Rawalpindi first.

Watched but no update:
- Airline airspace-reopen phrasing had only a KLM Tel Aviv suspension headline, not the specific "safely reopen before normal flights resume" condition; kept Iranian airspace first.
- Iran strike exact cities, hospital/school names, HIMARS missile type, Gulf missile distribution, US-troop base deaths, Hezbollah avenging name, Epstein subpoena, OpenAI investment, IPL opener, AWS disruption, and World Cup venue/cannot-participate questions had no direct Feb. 26 naming signal strong enough for another move.

## 2026-02-27 Update

Resolution events:
- Airline airspace-reopen condition resolved to Qatari airspace. I missed by anchoring on Iranian overflight disruption. Lesson: when the prompt says Gulf airlines and "normal operations", look for hub airspace, not only conflict-zone airspace.
- South Africa T20 semifinal opponent resolved to New Zealand. The late reduction still kept New Zealand first and was correct.
- Boys' school missile-strike city resolved to Qazvin and girls' school strike city resolved to Minab. I missed both by overweighting major cities. Lesson: Iran school-strike exact locations can be provincial/smaller cities; do not leave all mass on Tehran/Isfahan/Mashhad/Kermanshah.
- Final-collapse venue resolved to Geneva. Lowering Geneva was still correct after new evidence but I should have left it dominant because Trump later statements can simplify a sequence of talks to the best-known high-level venue.
- Multiple-explosions city resolved to Tehran and World Cup cannot-participate country resolved to Iran, both matching the leading forecasts.

Updates made:
- OpenAI investment chief executive: Feb. 27 funding articles confirmed SoftBank and Nvidia each investing $30bn, while Amazon invests $50bn. Kept Masayoshi Son first but added Jensen Huang as a live $30bn CEO alternative.
- Saudi/US basing: Reuters/Straits Times satellite imagery showed 13 KC-135 Stratotankers at Saudi Arabia's Prince Sultan Airbase, a US-used base, despite Saudi assurances to Iran. Raised Saudi Arabia sharply for the KC-135 damage country and base-access trade-threat markets, and increased Saudi weight for Gulf missile distribution, US troop base deaths, F-15E friendly-fire country, and the Iranian launch-area accusation.
- Epstein subpoena: Daily Beast/CBS/Al Jazeera reporting focused on Howard Lutnick after a removed DOJ Epstein-file photo, with committee members saying they may have enough support to force his testimony. Raised Lutnick above Pam Bondi for the House Oversight subpoena market.
- World Cup venue markets: after Iran resolved as the country saying it cannot participate, corrected the venue-change country forecast toward actual 2026 host countries Canada and Mexico rather than Qatar/UAE. Also reframed the cited conflict toward "US-Israel war on Iran" and "US-Iran conflict" while keeping Israel-Iran conflict live.
- Pakistan-Afghanistan target city: Pakistan struck Kabul, Kandahar and Paktia, while Afghan attacks remained focused on Pakistani border/KP positions. Shifted the Taliban-drone city market from Lahore toward Rawalpindi and Peshawar.
- Ukraine counteroffensive region: Al Jazeera reported Ukraine had regained 300-400 sq km in southern Ukraine since late January. Added Kherson and Zaporizhzhia ahead of Sumy/Kharkiv.
- Nepal prime minister: an NDTV/AFP profile made Gagan Thapa a serious PM hopeful after he took over Nepali Congress leadership. Rebalanced Balendra Shah and Gagan Thapa nearly even.

Watched but no update:
- Iran hospital/school follow-ons, Israeli town deaths, HIMARS missile type, Hezbollah avenging name, Germany evacuation-charter destination, AWS disruption country, Sri Lanka men's cricket coach, IPL opener, OpenAI bill name, and AFC women's quarterfinals had no direct Feb. 27 naming signal strong enough for a fresh move.

## 2026-02-28 Update

Resolution events:
- Israeli town with nine deaths resolved to Beit Shemesh. I missed by staying on Rehovot/Beersheba. Lesson: Israeli impact-location questions can resolve to Jerusalem-area commuter towns, not just better-known southern or central cities.
- Iran temporary council / Guardian Council member resolved to Ayatollah Alireza Arafi. I had Arafi as a tail but underweighted him. Lesson: when succession/legal-institution markets mention clerical councils, include senior seminary/Guardian Council figures early and do not overfocus on Ahmad Jannati.
- HIMARS missile resolved to PrSM / Precision Strike Missile. The expanded name was accepted, but exact acronym variants are still worth including when available.
- Gulf country receiving most missiles/drones resolved to UAE. I had UAE but behind Qatar. Lesson: in Gulf retaliation markets, UAE/Abu Dhabi/Dubai can dominate even when Qatar has the largest US base.
- Tehran hospital hit resolved to Gandhi Hospital, US troop base location resolved to Kuwait, Hezbollah avenging name resolved to Ali Khamenei, Palestinian village resolved to Abu Falah, Iran football captain asylum resolved to Zahra Ghanbari, and Iran WC venue-change conflict resolved to US-Israel war. I missed several by using broad high-salience guesses. Lesson: use concise exact phrases close to likely article wording; "US-Israel war" beats longer paraphrases.

Updates made:
- War activation changed the Gulf and Iran priors materially. Feb. 28 articles reported US-Israel strikes on Iran, Iranian missile/drone retaliation across UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, one death in Abu Dhabi, Dubai/Palm Jumeirah incidents, Bahrain Fifth Fleet targeting, Kuwait Ali al-Salem targeting, and explosions over Riyadh. Updated UAE/AWS, Gulf oil/refinery, Saudi projectile, embassy-evacuation, F-15E friendly-fire, and base-access markets accordingly.
- Indian trade coverage said the Iran conflict was already hitting Indian exporters, especially basmati rice flows, and wider India trade routes. Raised India over Pakistan for the fruit-exporter/shipping-disruption market, while leaving Pakistan live because the prompt is fruit-specific.
- Hormuz coverage intensified across several Feb. 28 pieces. Raised Strait of Hormuz further for the projectile-hit cargo ship market.
- Iraq reporting said the Jurf al-Nasr/Jurf al-Sakher base mostly hosts Kataeb Hezbollah and that Kataeb members were killed/wounded in airstrikes. Raised Kataib/Kataeb Hezbollah sharply for the northern Iraq militia-casualty market, hedging the transliteration.
- Iranian broadcaster/headquarters question now points most strongly to IRIB after Israel-US strikes and state-broadcaster references. Raised IRIB and the formal Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting name.
- Hospital-babies evacuation moved toward Tehran after the Gandhi Hospital resolution and Tehran-heavy strike coverage, while keeping smaller Iranian cities live after Qazvin/Minab school-strike lessons.

Watched but no update:
- Germany charter destinations, Pakistan/Taliban drone city, DHS replacement, OpenAI investment CEO, Epstein subpoena, Sri Lanka cricket coach, and Nepal prime minister had no new Feb. 28 exact-name signal stronger than existing priors.

## 2026-03-01 Update

Resolution events:
- Fruit-exporter losses resolved to South Africa. I missed by overreacting to India trade/rice coverage. Lesson: when the prompt says "fruit exporters", do not substitute adjacent commodity coverage; include major Southern Hemisphere fruit exporters when shipping lanes are disrupted.
- AWS region disruption resolved to Bahrain. I had Bahrain only as a tail. Lesson: AWS Middle East/Bahrain region can be the answer even when UAE and Israel have higher conflict salience.
- Taliban March drone city resolved to Rawalpindi. The late shift to Rawalpindi/Peshawar was useful and Rawalpindi deserved the top slot.
- Germany charter country resolved to Oman. I missed by anchoring on Jordan/Turkey evacuation routes. Lesson: Oman is not just a mediator; it can become the practical evacuation hub in US-Iran escalation questions.
- F-15E friendly-fire country resolved to Kuwait. Raising Kuwait on Feb. 28 helped but was still too low after the Ali al-Salem signal. Lesson: once one Kuwait base-casualty market resolves to Kuwait, related air-defense/friendly-fire markets should move more aggressively.
- Gulf energy-strike location resolved to Ras Laffan. I missed by staying on Saudi oil infrastructure. Lesson: Qatar/Ras Laffan should be live for Gulf energy markets whenever Qatar is heavily targeted.
- Broadcaster headquarters resolved to IRIB. The formal-name hedge helped, but short exact acronym "IRIB" was sufficient.

Updates made:
- Jebel Ali/Dubai: Al Jazeera and Straits Times reported plumes/smoke at Dubai's Jebel Ali Port after debris from an intercepted missile. Shifted the UAE emirate oil-facility/debris market from Abu Dhabi to Dubai.
- Hormuz tanker: Al Jazeera reported a Palau-flagged oil tanker ablaze near the Strait of Hormuz after an unidentified projectile. Raised Strait of Hormuz to a near-direct forecast.
- Tehran hospital: Reuters/NDTV/HT reported Gandhi Hospital in Tehran was hit. Raised Tehran for the hospital-babies evacuation city while leaving small-city tails because school-strike questions resolved to Minab/Qazvin.
- Qatar/Ras Laffan spillover: the Ras Laffan resolution and Qatar industrial-area fire made Qatar much more plausible for the remaining state-run refinery and plant/power-water facility markets.
- Oman mediation and exposure: Trump said Iran's new leadership wanted talks; HT reported Iran's offer via Oman, and Oman/Duqm/Muscat appeared in attack and embassy-shelter reporting. Raised Oman for Pakistan talks, Trump intermediary, Germany-style evacuation logic, and Oman/Muscat embassy/plant markets.
- Sri Lanka coach: Reuters/Straits Times reported Sanath Jayasuriya intends to step down after the T20 World Cup. Cut Jayasuriya and raised Mahela Jayawardene, Kumar Sangakkara, Marvan Atapattu and Naveed Nawaz for the next men's coach.
- Carrick/Manchester United: United remained unbeaten after beating Crystal Palace. With possible March fixtures before the 14th, moved the first-defeat market toward Liverpool, with "No team" and West Ham/Southampton live.
- School-strike responsibility: March 1 coverage continued to frame the Minab school strike as Israeli/US-Israeli bombing. Raised Israeli military and Israel in the school-strike responsibility/accusation markets.
- OpenAI: fresh funding coverage kept Masayoshi Son/SoftBank as the best $30bn CEO answer; raised him further while leaving Jensen Huang live for Nvidia's parallel $30bn commitment.

Watched but no update:
- DHS replacement, Epstein subpoena, prediction-market co-sponsor/bill-name, Tehran southwest school town, KC-135 damage country, Iranian island bombing, AFC women's fixtures, Ukraine counteroffensive region, and airline financial outlook had no March 1 exact-answer signal strong enough for another move.

import pandas as pd
# quick verification of currently active soonest rows after submission is via harness, CSV stays stale until next refresh.

act=df[~df.is_resolved].copy(); act['rd']=pd.to_datetime(act.resolution_date, errors='coerce')
print('active',len(act),'resolved',df.is_resolved.sum(),'empty aggregate',act.market_aggregate.isna().sum())
print(act.sort_values(['rd','qid'])[['qid','resolution_date','title']].head(12).to_string(index=False, max_colwidth=140))
PY"
